publications.TITLE: Selection of extra-early white quality protein maize (Zea mays L.) inbred lines for drought and low soil nitrogen resilient hybrid production
publications.AUTHORS: Benjamin Annor and Baffour Badu-Apraku
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Bagauda, Ikenne, Mokwa, Ile-Ife)
publications.METHODS: Field trials with 96 QPM inbred lines and 4 checks conducted in multi-location trials under drought, low nitrogen, and optimal conditions. Traits evaluated included grain yield, days to silking, plant height, ears per plant, stay-green rating, and more. A base index (BI) was used to assess stress resilience. Statistical methods included ANOVA, heritability estimation, and GGE biplot analysis to evaluate genotype performance and stability.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Targeted combined drought and low soil nitrogen resilience for maize in SSA. Emphasizes cultivar development to overcome dual climate-related stressors.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
57 out of 96 inbred lines showed resilience under combined drought and low nitrogen conditions. 

TZEEQI 7 and TZEEQI 60 were the most stable and high-yielding. 

Grain yield positively correlated with ears per plant and negatively with anthesis-silking interval and leaf death. 

Traits had moderate to high heritability, indicating potential for selection. 

Stress-resilient QPM inbreds can improve food security and reduce malnutrition in SSA.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Stress trials conducted in 2012; while relevant, newer environmental variations not accounted for. No direct socioeconomic assessment included.
publications.NOTES: Backed by Gates Foundation and Stress Tolerant Maize for Africa (STMA) initiative. Data available via IITA CKAN repository.

publications.TITLE: Progress in understanding Pseudocercospora banana pathogens and the development of resistant Musa germplasm
publications.AUTHORS: A. E. Alakonya, J. Kimunye, G. Mahuku, D. Amah, B. Uwimana, A. Brown, R. Swennen
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tropical and Subtropical regions (focus on Africa, Asia, and Latin America)
publications.IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Uganda, IITA Tanzania
publications.METHODS: Narrative literature review synthesizing pathogen biology, climate impacts, host-pathogen interactions, genomics, and effector protein research related to Pseudocercospora pathogens in banana. Highlights breeding strategies, including molecular tools, resistant germplasm, and transformation protocols.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Explores how climate change may shift Pseudocercospora pathogen distributions, intensify disease severity, and affect resistance durability. Models suggest temperature increases could expand banana growing zones but raise water and pathogen pressure.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Pseudocercospora pathogens are spreading and evolving under climate pressure. 

Conventional fungicides are costly and lose effectiveness. 

Genomic and transformation approaches offer tools for breeding resistance. 

Resistant germplasm (e.g., NARITA, FHIA, PITA lines) are in use but more are needed. 

Marker-assisted selection, transcriptomics, and RNAi technologies can support future resistance strategies.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Synthesis based on secondary data; practical implementation of genetic transformation and screening strategies varies across programs and contexts.
publications.NOTES: Funded by USAID and CGIAR Roots, Tubers and Bananas (RTB). Highlights knowledge gaps in pathogen-host dynamics and need for multi-environment resistance screening protocols.

publications.TITLE: Prevalence of Aflatoxin- and Fumonisin-Producing Fungi Associated with Cereal Crops Grown in Zimbabwe and Their Associated Risks in a Climate Change Scenario
publications.AUTHORS: Juliet Akello, Alejandro Ortega-Beltran, Bwalya Katati, Joseph Atehnkeng, Joao Augusto, Chama M. Mwila, George Mahuku, David Chikoye, Ranajit Bandyopadhyay
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Zimbabwe
publications.IITA OFFICE: IITA Zambia, IITA Nigeria, IITA Malawi, IITA Mozambique, IITA Tanzania
publications.METHODS: Field sampling and laboratory analysis of 980 cereal grain samples (maize, sorghum, finger millet, pearl millet) from 12 districts across 4 agroecological zones in Zimbabwe. Fungal identification, mycotoxin quantification (AF, FM), and exposure risk modeling were conducted. Analytical techniques included PDA culture, HPLC validation, and Bayesian risk modeling.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Highlights climate change as a key driver of mycotoxin risk, with hotter and drier zones linked to higher aflatoxin levels, while wetter seasons increase fumonisin prevalence. Recommends dietary diversification as a climate adaptation strategy.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Maize had high prevalence of AF (52%) and FM (89%), with significant exceedances of health safety limits. 

Small grains were much safer. High mycotoxin levels linked to drought and storage conditions. 

Maize consumption poses elevated liver cancer and toxicity risks, especially for children. 

Diversifying diets with small grains could reduce public health risks.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not assess chronic exposure over multiple years; focused on Zimbabwe only; FM regulation levels are lacking in some contexts.
publications.NOTES: First national study assessing small grain mycotoxin risks in Zimbabwe. Supports regional policy reforms for grain safety and storage.

publications.TITLE: Physiological responses of cowpea simultaneously exposed to water deficit stress and varying light intensities at vegetative and reproductive growth stages
publications.AUTHORS: O. I. Adeniyi, S. A. Adejumo, M. Fofana, F. T. Adegbehingbe
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
publications.METHODS: Pot experiment in a randomized complete block design with factorial arrangement. Two cowpea varieties were subjected to combinations of water deficit stress (four levels) and three levels of light intensity at vegetative and reproductive stages. Physiological parameters (photosynthesis, stomatal conductance, transpiration, chlorophyll content, leaf temperature) and yield traits (pod number, grain weight, biomass) were recorded and analyzed via ANOVA and Duncanâ€™s test.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study directly investigates how light and drought stress, worsened by climate change, affect physiological and yield performance of cowpea. Demonstrates importance of shade and water-efficient cropping practices under future climate stress.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
High light intensity combined with water deficit stress significantly reduced yield, photosynthesis, and transpiration in cowpea. 

Reduced light intensity improved tolerance to water stress and yield performance. 

IT99K-573-1-1 variety showed greater resilience. 

Vegetative-stage stress had more severe impacts than reproductive-stage stress.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Study was pot-based and may not fully replicate field-scale conditions. Environmental controls were artificial, and soil type was localized.
publications.NOTES: Supports selection and development of drought- and heat-tolerant cowpea varieties suited to low-input environments in West Africa.

publications.TITLE: The power of narratives: Explaining inaction on gender mainstreaming in Ugandaâ€™s climate change policy
publications.AUTHORS: Mariola Acosta, Margit van Wessel, Severine van Bommel, Edidah L. Ampaire, Laurence Jassogne, Peter H. Feindt
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda
publications.METHODS: Narrative policy analysis based on 30 semi-structured expert interviews and 10 multi-stakeholder meetings. The study identifies 22 policy stories and five narrative frames used by Ugandan policymakers to engage with or resist gender mainstreaming in climate adaptation policy. Atlas.ti was used to code and group story elements.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs; Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Examines how climate change policy in Uganda includes gender mainstreaming on paper, but faces ideological, cultural, and political resistance in practice. Narratives reveal how climate discourse intersects with gender resistance and inaction.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Gender mainstreaming in Ugandaâ€™s climate policy is undermined by competing narratives. 

While the Gender Equality narrative is dominant, it is often weakened by shifts toward narratives of ignorance, structural inertia, male supremacy, and reconciliatory approaches. 

Narrative shifts result in blame-shifting, legitimization of inaction, and resource diversion, ultimately limiting gender transformation.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Potential interviewer effect due to international and gender identity of the lead researcher; study reflects narrated stories rather than direct policy outcomes.
publications.NOTES: Highlights ideational power in shaping resistance to gender policy in climate adaptation; relevant to broader gender policy efforts beyond Uganda.

publications.TITLE: Variations in yield gaps of smallholder cocoa systems and the main determining factors along a climate gradient in Ghana
publications.AUTHORS: Issaka Abdulai, Munir P. Hoffmann, Laurence Jassogne, Richard Asare, Sophie Graefe, Hsiao-Hang Tao, Sander Muilerman, Philippe Vaast, Piet Van Asten, Peter LÃ¤derach, Reimund P. RÃ¶tter
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (climate gradient zones)
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate suitability zones for cocoa and adaptation potential through sustainable intensification, highlighting drought-prone areas and the need for crop diversification in marginal zones.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Yield gaps in cocoa are strongly influenced by zone-specific climatic and socioeconomic factors. 

In dry zones, older farmers and limited labor contribute to lower productivity. 

In wet zones, training and hybrid varieties help reduce yield gaps. 

Sustainable intensification is recommended in suitable zones to offset losses in marginal areas.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Lack of long-term yield trial data; reliance on farmer-reported data; challenges in isolating biophysical and socioeconomic effects due to zone interactions.
publications.NOTES: Part of CGIAR Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and Forest, Trees and Agroforestry (FTA) programs.

publications.TITLE: Adaptation Strategies to Climate Change and Impacts on Household Income and Food Security: Evidence from Sahelian Region of Niger
publications.AUTHORS: Seydou Zakari, Germaine Ibro, Bokar Moussa, Tahirou Abdoulaye
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Niger (Regions: Dosso, TillabÃ©ri, Maradi, Zinder)
publications.METHODS: Used data from 1,783 rural households in Niger. Employed binary logit regression to identify determinants of six climate adaptation strategies and used Propensity Score Matching (PSM) to evaluate the impacts on household income and food security.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong adaptation focus; explores perceptions of climate variability and household-level responses to climate shocks (e.g., rainfall variability, droughts).
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Crop and income diversification, changing planting times, and drought-tolerant crops are key strategies. 

Adoption improves household income (by ~7,700 FCFA) and food security (7â€“9% more likely to be food secure). 

Credit access, extension services, and climate information are key enabling factors.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: PSM does not account for unobserved bias; findings are based on reported and observed variables only.
publications.NOTES: Religious and spiritual beliefs influence climate perceptions. Radio/TV is the main source of climate info. The study suggests scaling up institutional support to enhance resilience.

publications.TITLE: Impacts of climate variability and food price volatility on household income and food security of farm households in East and West Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Tesfamicheal Wossen, Thomas Berger, Mekbib G. Haile, Christian Troost
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ethiopia and Ghana
publications.METHODS: Used agent-based modeling (MPMAS) to simulate the impacts of climate and price variability on farm household income and food security. Model integrated household-level data, DSSAT and FAO yield models, climate and CPI records, and simulated adaptation strategies (credit, off-farm income, fertilizer subsidy, improved seed).
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Direct focus on the impact of climate variability (rainfall, temperature) and its interaction with price shocks. Also evaluates adaptation effectiveness under these stressors.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate and price variability significantly reduce income and increase food insecurity, especially for poorer households. 

Policy-supported adaptation measures improve income and food security, but benefits are uneven. 

Multi-strategy packages outperform single measures.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not model safety nets or kinship-based coping; adaptation costs not fully assessed; simulations rely on stylized scenarios and aggregate CPI categories.
publications.NOTES: Stresses need for integrated, tailored adaptation packages and policies that consider inequality. Highlights the role of livestock as coping assets and the limitations of self-reliance in the face of compound shocks.

publications.TITLE: Smallholder farmersâ€™ perceptions of and adaptations to climate change in the Nigerian savanna
publications.AUTHORS: Justice Akpene Tambo and Tahirou Abdoulaye
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigerian savanna (specifically Borno State: Sudan savanna and Northern Guinea savanna)
publications.METHODS: Primary household survey with 200 smallholder farmers across two agro-ecological zones (AEZs). Complemented by focus group discussions (FGDs). Data analyzed using descriptive statistics and Mannâ€“Whitney U tests to assess differences in adaptation practices between AEZs. The study investigates climate perceptions, adaptation behaviors, and barriers to adaptation.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the central theme of the article. Adaptation is discussed extensively, with emphasis on drought response, variable rainfall, and the need for planned interventions. Mitigation is not mentioned.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
92% of farmers perceive increasing temperature; 84% perceive reduced and erratic rainfall.

Adaptation practices include early-maturing crops, mixed cropping, planting trees, and shifting planting dates.

Adaptation capacity is limited by lack of credit, information, and irrigation infrastructure.

Only ~15% of respondents reported no adaptation; better than most regions in Africa due to donor projects.

Irrigation and agricultural insurance are underutilized but potentially transformative.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of reliable meteorological data limited trend validation

Self-reported adaptation may include habitual behaviors rather than new responses

Study limited to one Nigerian state (Borno); results not generalizable across all savanna regions
publications.NOTES:
Acknowledges IITA and DTMA support for drought-tolerant maize adoption

Suggests index-based insurance and irrigation support as future priorities

Strong connection to farmer memory recall and context-specific responses to climate threats

publications.TITLE: Between rainfall and food poverty: Assessing vulnerability to climate change in an agricultural economy
publications.AUTHORS: Olawale Emmanuel Olayide, Tunrayo Alabi
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (National-level study disaggregated by state)
publications.METHODS: The study used geo-referenced rainfall data (1970â€“2015) and food poverty data from Nigeriaâ€™s National Bureau of Statistics to assess the relationship between rainfall variability and food poverty. It applied GIS mapping, decadal trend analysis, and statistical association tests (e.g., Chi-square) to examine vulnerability across regions and identify policy-relevant hotspots for climate adaptation.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The studyâ€™s central theme is vulnerability to climate variability, especially rainfall extremes (droughts, floods), and its implications for food systems in rain-fed agriculture.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
High rainfall variability correlates with high food poverty, especially in northern Nigeria.

Regions with low variability (e.g., FCT, Niger) also tend to have lower food poverty.

The study supports using spatial data for adaptive policy targeting.

Suggests integrating early warning systems and climate-smart agriculture to address vulnerability.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of integrated socioeconomic data limits nuanced poverty interpretation

Results may not capture non-climatic drivers of food insecurity

Focuses on rainfall only; does not account for temperature or other climate variables
publications.NOTES:
Offers a replicable method for spatial targeting of climate adaptation efforts

Highlights Nigeriaâ€™s increasing vulnerability to climatic shocks in both the north (drought) and south (floods)

Grounded in national policy relevance (SDGs 1, 2, and 13)

publications.TITLE: Phenotypic approaches to drought in cassava: review
publications.AUTHORS: Emmanuel Okogbenin, Tim L. Setter, Morag Ferguson, Rose Mutegi, Hernan Ceballos, Bunmi Olasanmi, Martin Fregene
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, Caribbean, and Asia â€“ with field study references from Nigeria, Tanzania, Kenya, and Colombia
publications.METHODS: This is a methods review synthesizing over three decades of cassava drought research. It evaluates field phenotyping methods, physiological traits, experimental design (e.g., use of water table gradients), and new tools like marker-assisted selection (MAS), marker-assisted recurrent selection (MARS), and genomic selection. It proposes early-growth phase phenotyping protocols to accelerate breeding for drought tolerance.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Cassavaâ€™s resilience under drought is the core theme, with site-specific strategies for measuring tolerance. Emphasizes phenotyping under real-world stress environments like Minjibir, Kano (Nigeria).
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cassava tolerates drought via mechanisms like early leaf shedding, deep rooting, and stem starch storage.

Modern breeding needs rapid, cost-effective phenotyping (e.g., bulking at 7 MAP, early PI, stem starch).

MARS and GS offer new ways to select for complex traits without full-cycle evaluation.

Early bulking and stem starch content are predictive of yield under stress.

Field phenotyping can be designed without irrigation by using environmental gradients (e.g., water table depth).
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Late-stage traits are hard to phenotype efficiently.

Limited data on field-ready, high-throughput phenotyping tools.

Challenges in controlling drought conditions without irrigation.

Need for more integration between physiological data and genomic selection.
publications.NOTES:
Highlights collaboration between IITA, CIAT, and national systems.

Introduces modified early-phase phenotyping for breeding efficiency.

Suggests cassava breeding could benefit from tools used in maize and rice.

Emphasizes value of African breeding programs using IITA germplasm in drought-prone zones.

publications.TITLE: Reviewing research priorities in weed ecology, evolution and management: a horizon scan
publications.AUTHORS: P. Neve, J. N. Barney, Y. Buckley, R. D. Cousens, S. Graham, N. R. Jordan, A. Lawton-Rauh, M. Liebman, M. B. Mesgaran, M. Schut, et al.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: n/a
publications.METHODS: The authors conducted a global horizon scanning exercise involving 35 experts from weed ecology, weed science, invasion biology, and social science. Prior to a 2014 workshop in Spain, participants submitted 3â€“5 key research questions. These were compiled into 124 questions, ranked through group discussion, and synthesized into 30 top priorities grouped under seven themes. These themes were then analyzed and contextualized in a transdisciplinary commentary.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate changeâ€™s role in weed range shifts, invasiveness, and evolution. Emphasizes adaptation needs and risk from intensifying weed pressure under changing climates.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Seven priority themes: transdisciplinary research, weed evolution, climate change, invasiveness, agroecology, adoption of IWM, and future of weed science.

Major barriers to IWM adoption include reliance on simple technologies and lack of socioeconomic engagement.

Calls for a systems approach integrating public-private partnerships, ecological resilience, and broader agroecosystem thinking.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Horizon scan reflects perspectives from a specific academic network.

Rankings are consensus-based and not statistically validated.

Some questions could not be cleanly categorized.
publications.NOTES:
Recommends repositioning weed research within broader ecological and food systems debates.

Emphasizes the importance of multifunctionality in weed research, including both positive and negative ecosystem roles.

A rare example of interdisciplinary agenda-setting within agricultural science.

publications.TITLE: Structural approaches to modeling the impact of climate change and adaptation technologies on crop yields and food security
publications.AUTHORS: Shahnila Islam, Nicola Cenacchi, Timothy B. Sulser, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Guy Hareau, Ulrich Kleinwechter, Daniel Mason-D'Croz, Swamikannu Nedumaran, Richard Robertson, Sherman Robinson, Keith Wiebe
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with regional modeling examples in Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Latin America, and others)
publications.METHODS: This paper reviews the â€œstructural approachâ€ that integrates crop simulation, physiological, and economic modeling to assess the impact of climate change and adaptation technologies. A case study within the paper applies this framework to analyze drought- and heat-tolerant crop varieties using DSSAT and the IMPACT economic model. It evaluates exogenous (biophysical) and endogenous (market-adjusted) yield outcomes for multiple regions and crops.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. The paper highlights the uncertainty and complexity of yield responses under RCP 8.5 and emphasizes the importance of stress-resilient technologies for future food security.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Structural modeling integrates climate, crop, and economic models

Drought- and heat-tolerant varieties can offset some negative yield impacts under climate change

Market feedback reduces the full yield benefit of technologies, but still results in positive food security outcomes

Adoption modeling varies by region, crop, and technology suitability

Prices fall with increased supply, benefiting poor consumers
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Food access and utilization dimensions of food security not included

Results vary depending on adoption rates, climate severity, and crop model calibration

Costs of technology development/adoption not modeled
publications.NOTES:
Conducted under the CGIAR Global Futures and Strategic Foresight (GFSF) program

Supports use of ex-ante modeling for climate-resilient agricultural investments

publications.TITLE: Smallholder farmers in eastern Africa and climate change: a review of risks and adaptation options with implications for future adaptation programmes
publications.AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, Jared Serem, Clare Stirling, Florence Kyazze, Maren Radeny, Michael Misiko, Songporne Tongruksawattana, Lydia Nafula, Mercy Gakii, Kai Sonder
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Eastern Africa (Kenya, Ethiopia, Uganda, Tanzania)
publications.METHODS: The study uses a realist literature review combined with two case studies in Kenya to identify climate risks, smallholder adaptation strategies, and institutional barriers. It includes 36 empirical studies, national climate reports, and CGIAR/CCAFS site-level baseline surveys, synthesizing adaptation responses by AEZ, proximity to cities, and institutional support.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper is focused entirely on climate change impacts and adaptation. It maps regional risks (less rainfall, temperature rise, erratic rain) and examines social, economic, and environmental factors that shape farmer responses.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Decreased rainfall and rising temperature are the most reported risks across East Africa

Farmers use diverse strategies: crop timing changes, tree planting, conservation agriculture, selling assets

Adaptation capacity is higher near major cities

Constraints include land size, credit access, institutional support, and misperceptions of climate risks

Collaborative programs (CBOs, extension, NGOs) improve adoption of robust strategies
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Farmer climate perceptions donâ€™t always match meteorological data

Case studies may not be generalizable

Survey tools varied in depth and focus
publications.NOTES:
Highlights importance of gender-aware programming and localized adaptation strategies

Recommends integrating indigenous knowledge with scientific forecasts for better targeting of adaptation policies

publications.TITLE: Coffea arabica yields decline in Tanzania due to climate change: Global implications
publications.AUTHORS: A.C.W. Craparo, P.J.A. Van Asten, P. LÃ¤derach, L.T.P. Jassogne, S.W. Grab
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern highlands of Tanzania (Mt. Kilimanjaro, Mt. Meru, and Tanga/Ngorongoro Crater regions)
publications.METHODS: The study used long-term time series data (1962â€“2010) on coffee yields and climatic variables from the Tanzanian highlands. It applied ARIMA and non-linear (logistic) regression models to assess the impact of rising minimum night-time temperatures (Tmin) on C. arabica yields. The authors also compared Tmin trends with those in other major coffee-growing countries and projected future impacts under RCP 6.0 scenarios.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focused explicitly on climate change impactsâ€”specifically minimum temperature increasesâ€”on arabica coffee yields in Tanzania and globally.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Tmin rose by 0.31Â°C per decade, correlating with yield declines of 137 Â± 16.87 kg/ha per Â°C increase.

ARIMA model forecasts yields dropping to 145 Â± 41 kg/ha by 2060 if trends continue without adaptation.

Logistic regression predicts yields may fall below 100 kg/ha under continued warming.

Rainfall variables had limited explanatory power compared to temperature trends.

The impact of shade trees may be context-dependent and not always beneficial at higher Tmin.

Similar warming trends are emerging in Ethiopia, Kenya, Colombia, Brazil, and Costa Ricaâ€”posing global risks to arabica production.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Yield data uncertainty due to smallholder production systems

Limitations of linear and ARIMA models for long-term biological projections

Lack of site-specific socio-economic data and non-climatic stressor modeling
publications.NOTES:
The study provides the first quantitative global evidence that climate change has already reduced arabica yields.

Suggests that adaptation will require moving coffee production upslope, which may threaten biodiversity conservation areas.

publications.TITLE: Establishing Optimal Planting Windows for Contrasting Sorghum Cultivars across Diverse Agro-Ecologies of North-Eastern Nigeria: A Modelling Approach
publications.AUTHORS: Folorunso M. Akinseye, Hakeem A. Ajeigbe, Alpha Y. Kamara, Akinrotimi O. Omotayo, Abdullahi I. Tofa, Anthony M. Whitbread
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: North-Eastern Nigeria (Sudan Savanna, Northern Guinea Savanna, Southern Guinea Savanna)
publications.METHODS: Calibrated and validated APSIM crop model using field experiments and multi-locational on-farm trials. Simulated sorghum grain yield under different planting windows (May 16â€“July 15) for five cultivars across 33 sites and 3 AEZs from 1985 to 2010. Evaluated phenology, leaf area index, and water use efficiency under climate variability and soil fertility differences.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on optimizing sowing periods and cultivar suitability as adaptation to delayed rains and terminal droughts. Simulates outcomes under long-term climate variability using location-specific weather and soil data.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal planting windows varied by cultivar and agroecology. 

Early sowing (Mayâ€“June) improved yield and resilience. 

Photoperiod-sensitive varieties required tighter windows. 

Improved Deko and ICSV400 performed best across zones. 

Simulation showed significant yield gains from aligning cultivar phenology with rainfall onset.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Model does not include phosphorus stress; on-farm data varied in management intensity; yield estimates rely on downscaled climate data.
publications.NOTES: Supports climate-smart decision-making for sorghum intensification. Combines APSIM modeling, field trials, and policy relevance. Funded through CGIAR and TAMASA initiative.

publications.TITLE: Unveiling African rainforest composition and vulnerability to global change
publications.AUTHORS: Maxime RÃ©jou-MÃ©chain, FrÃ©dÃ©ric Mortier, Jean-FranÃ§ois Bastin, Guillaume Cornu, Nicolas Barbier, et al.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Africa â€“ primarily Cameroon, Central African Republic, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Republic of the Congo, and Gabon
publications.METHODS: This large-scale ecological modeling study analyzes a dataset of over 6 million trees across ~185,000 forest plots in Central Africa using supervised component generalized linear regression (SCGLR). It models taxonomic and functional composition, classifies forest types, and assesses their vulnerability to climate and anthropogenic changes projected through 2085.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the study, which uses downscaled projections under RCP 4.5 to forecast exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity of forest communities, applying the IPCC framework for ecosystem vulnerability.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Forest composition varies along climate and disturbance gradients.

Vulnerability is highest at the forest margins and in much of DRC.

Human disturbances reduce phylogenetic diversity and adaptive capacity.

Functional traits (e.g., wood density, maximum diameter) correlate with vulnerability.

New floristic and functional maps support conservation planning and prioritization.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Potential underestimation of future anthropogenic pressures (e.g., road expansion not modeled)

Uncertainty in climate projections over Central Africa due to sparse meteorological data

Some forest types are poorly represented in existing protected areas
publications.NOTES:
Data and code are publicly available for replication

Aligns with conservation policy needs in transboundary forest regions

Highlights role of human-induced disturbance in driving rapid forest composition shifts

publications.TITLE: Preparing for, coping with and bouncing back after shocks. A nuanced resilience assessment for smallholder farms and farmers in Northern Ghana
publications.AUTHORS: Mirja Michalscheck, Fred Kizito, Bekele H. Kotu, Franklin K. Avornyo, Carl Timler, Jeroen C. J. Groot
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern Ghana (Duko village, Tamale area)
publications.METHODS: The study combined the FarmDESIGN whole-farm model with participatory consultations and interviews in Duko village to assess the vulnerability, coping strategies, and resilience of low-, medium-, and high-resource endowed smallholder farms. Four shock scenarios were tested: drought, pest infestation (fall armyworm), price shock, and labor shock. Farmer-specific responses and gendered coping strategies were also analyzed.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is anchored in addressing climate-related shocks (drought, pests) and designing resilience strategies suited to smallholdersâ€™ realities.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought was the most damaging shock across all farm types

Low-resource farms had very limited recovery capacity without external support

Gendered roles significantly shaped coping strategies (e.g., shea nut processing by women)

Adoption of Africa RISING technologies enhanced resilience but with variation across farm types

Expansion of land or livestock was a common strategy, with potential ecological trade-offs

Farmer resilience was often more linked to social and financial capital than just technologies
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
FarmDESIGN is static and does not simulate long-term changes

Model results are based on assumptions from a specific year (2015)

Recovery capacity is contextual and may not be generalizable beyond Duko
publications.NOTES:
Gender-specific strategies were especially insightful (e.g., income from shea, dawadawa, susu groups)

Social cohesion and access to micro-finance were important resilience factors

The project linked closely with Africa RISINGâ€™s technology park and on-farm trials

publications.TITLE: Drought vulnerability of central Sahel agrosystems: a modelling-approach based on magnitudes of changes and machine learning techniques
publications.AUTHORS: Ismaguil HanadÃ© Houmma, Loubna El Mansouri, SÃ©bastien Gadal, Elhousna Faouzi, Amadou Abdourhamane Toure, Maman Garba, Yasmina Imani, Moha El-Ayachi, Rachid Hadria
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Sahel (Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso)
publications.METHODS: The study introduces the Multivariate Agricultural Drought Vulnerability Index (MADVI) using machine learning (SVM, RF, KNN, NB) to assess drought risk across the Central Sahel. It uses 28 biophysical, climatic, socioeconomic, and physiographic factors derived from satellite data (1981â€“2021) and PCA to model and map drought exposure and agrosystem vulnerability.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central; the paper documents rising temperature anomalies and climate water deficits that disproportionately impact the Sahelâ€™s farming systems.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
28 vulnerability factors modeled with PCA and ML to predict agricultural drought risk

SVM and RF models had the highest prediction accuracy (ROC > 0.92)

Southwestern Niger, Mali, and Burkina Faso had the highest drought vulnerability

Socioeconomic factors (e.g., rural dependence, infrastructure) were the strongest predictors

Outputs can inform drought early warning systems and regional development planning
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No local adaptation or irrigation data available

Vulnerability only modeled from satellite and environmental data, no household-level validation

Model assumes linear relationships in some components
publications.NOTES:
First application of magnitude-of-change trend modeling in this context

Highlights relevance for informing early warning, planning, and climate adaptation priorities in vulnerable Sahelian states

publications.TITLE: Climate Change Adaptation Strategies and Its Impact on Household Vulnerability to Food Insecurity: A Micro-Level Evidence from Southwest Ethiopia
publications.AUTHORS: Andualem Begashaw, Mengistu Ketema, Abule Mehare, Mesay Yami, Shiferaw Feleke, Tahirou Abdoulaye
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Bench Maji Zone, Southwest Ethiopia
publications.METHODS: The study used a cross-sectional quantitative research design with multistage sampling to survey 390 smallholder households across four districts. It applied a multinomial endogenous treatment effect model to assess how adoption of various climate change adaptation strategiesâ€”individually and in combinationâ€”impacts household vulnerability to food insecurity. Vulnerability was measured using the Vulnerability as Expected Poverty (VEP) approach with food consumption expenditure as a proxy.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explicitly assesses the effectiveness of climate change adaptation strategiesâ€”such as crop management, soil and water conservation, and livelihood diversificationâ€”in improving food security under increasingly variable and extreme weather patterns.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Combined adaptation strategies significantly reduce household vulnerability to food insecurity.

The combination of crop management and soil/water conservation had the largest effect (24% reduction).

Livelihood diversification also contributed substantially to reducing food insecurity risk.

Education, perception of climate risk, and livestock ownership were positively associated with adoption.

A significant proportion of current food-secure households remain vulnerable to future insecurity, highlighting the need for sustained adaptation efforts.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Results are based on cross-sectional rather than longitudinal data

The food insecurity metric relies on expenditure rather than other multidimensional indicators

Potential omitted variables not captured in the survey (e.g., sociocultural dynamics)
publications.NOTES:
The study contributes to policy and NGO efforts by identifying specific strategies that most effectively reduce vulnerability.

IITAâ€™s support of this research underlines its focus on resilience and sustainable development in smallholder agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa.

publications.TITLE: Remotely-sensed slowing down in spatially patterned dryland ecosystems
publications.AUTHORS: Michiel P. Veldhuis, Ricardo Martinez-Garcia, Vincent Deblauwe, Vasilis Dakos
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sudan (Western Sector of Southern Kordofan State)
publications.METHODS: Used 20 years of satellite-derived vegetation and climate data (EVI and SPEI) over Sudanâ€™s rainfall gradient. Combined spatial vegetation pattern classification (gaps, labyrinths, spots) with temporal indicators of critical slowing down (autocorrelation, variance, responsiveness). Validated findings against model simulations of vegetation dynamics under aridity stress.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focused on resilience under increasing aridity. Emphasizes that vegetation responses slow as aridity increases, with transitions between spatial patterns linked to temporary system instability.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Patterns of vegetation (gaps â†’ labyrinths â†’ spots) are linked to changes in responsiveness, autocorrelation, and variance. 

These indicators do not always behave linearlyâ€”transient dynamics complicate their use as early warning tools.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Long transients may limit real-time utility of pattern-based indicators. Variability in rainfall and landscape heterogeneity were not fully captured in the model.
publications.NOTES: Suggests spatial self-organization may be useful for resilience monitoring, but transient effects mean these systems need long-term observation and caution in interpretation.

publications.TITLE: Managing vulnerability to drought and enhancing livelihood resilience in sub-Saharan Africa: Technological, institutional and policy options
publications.AUTHORS: Bekele Shiferaw, Kindie Tesfaye, Menale Kassie, Tsedeke Abate, B.M. Prasanna, Abebe Menkir
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (multiple regions, with special attention to East and Southern Africa)
publications.METHODS: This article synthesizes findings from various empirical studies and project experiences (notably Drought Tolerant Maize for Africa) to assess drought risk in SSA. It presents technological, institutional, and policy strategies for managing drought risk, enhancing resilience, and supporting sustainable intensification. It reviews ex-ante and ex-post coping strategies, climate-smart technologies (e.g., DT maize), conservation agriculture, and market-based instruments such as weather index insurance.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is framed as a primary intensifier of drought risk, and the article emphasizes proactive drought risk management, not just emergency response. Mitigation is mentioned in the context of CA and sustainable intensification.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought is the most severe climate risk in SSA, with both direct and indirect impacts.

Farmers often rely on ex-ante coping strategies that reduce income potential (e.g., low input use).

DT maize and CA are key tools for drought adaptation and mitigation.

Climate information, insurance, and market access must be part of a broader strategy.

Institutional innovations and integrated policies are more effective than isolated responses.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weak uptake of conservation agriculture due to long adoption timelines

Challenges scaling weather index insurance due to data and awareness constraints

Need for more site-specific policy and institutional adaptation strategies
publications.NOTES:
DT maize projected to lift 4 million people out of poverty

Authors advocate moving from crisis response to anticipatory drought risk management

Emphasizes integrated approaches across institutions, geographies, and farming systems

publications.TITLE: Diversity within village institutions and its implication for resilience in the context of climate change in Cameroon
publications.AUTHORS: H. Carolyn Peach Brown, Denis J. Sonwa
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (Centre, East, and Southwest provinces in the humid forest zone)
publications.METHODS: The study employed quantitative household surveys and key informant interviews in 13 villages across three provinces of Cameroon. Data from 232 respondents were analyzed to assess the structure, membership diversity, inter-group collaboration, and potential for social learning within local institutions, with a focus on implications for climate change adaptation. Statistical analysis included chi-square tests and linear regression.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity 

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explores the role of internal village institutions in building adaptive capacity and resilience to climate change, especially through social capital and learning mechanisms.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Nearly all villagers belong to at least one local institution, with high diversity in gender, education, and occupation.

These institutions are not yet directly addressing climate change but serve as platforms for future adaptation strategies.

Gendered patterns of participation reveal womenâ€™s strong roles in NTFP collection and savings groups, while men dominate marketing and forest exploitation groups.

Social capital and inter-group collaboration vary significantly across regions and influence adaptation potential.

Institutional diversity could enable more effective knowledge-sharing and resilience if supported through intentional capacity building.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No longitudinal data to track changes in institutions or adaptation behavior

Limited integration between village institutions and external climate adaptation programs

Gender and ethnic homogeneity in some villages may limit innovation
publications.NOTES:
The paper calls for a gender-sensitive, long-term process of facilitated learning and collaboration between local institutions and external actors.

Recognizes IITAâ€™s role via CoFCCA in linking rural institutions with regional adaptation knowledge.

publications.TITLE: Mediation and moderation roles of resilience capacity in the shockâ€“food-security nexus in northern Ghana
publications.AUTHORS: Isaac Gershon K. Ansah, Bekele Hundie Kotu, Julius Manda, Francis Muthoni, Carlo Azzarri
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern Ghana
publications.METHODS: Used two waves of household survey data (2014 and 2020) combined with satellite-based weather data to assess food security effects of drought and heat shocks. Applied correlated random effects (CRE), IV regression, and structural equation modeling to evaluate whether resilience capacity moderates or mediates shock effects. Resilience measured via RIMA indicators: access to basic services, assets, adaptive capacity, and social safety nets.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Resilience capacity mediates and moderates the negative impacts of drought and heat stress on food security. Shows how climate shocks undermine calorie intake, diet diversity, and food expenditure, but effects are reduced through adaptive capacity and safety nets.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Heat stress and drought reduce household calorie consumption and food diversity, but resilience capacity mitigates these effects. 

Mediation effects were stronger than moderation. 

Structural equation modeling revealed that 74% (drought) and 537% (heat) of food security impacts were mediated via resilience. 

Promotes investments in sustainable intensification and social protection to build resilience.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: CRE models rely on time-invariant assumptions; resilience indicators exclude psychosocial factors; mediation effects may vary across regions.
publications.NOTES: Supports Africa RISING and CGIAR resilience-building strategies. Uses robust econometrics to distinguish indirect vs. interaction effects.

publications.TITLE: Assessing Climate Smart Agriculture and Its Determinants of Practice in Ghana: A Case of the Cocoa Production System
publications.AUTHORS: Felix Akrofi-Atitianti, Chinwe Ifejika Speranza, Louis Bockel, Richard Asare
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Juabeso and Atwima Mponua districts)
publications.METHODS: Data collected using household surveys (n=80), focus group discussions, and expert interviews. Compared CSA/Agroecology and conventional cocoa practices using farm budget analysis, FAO EX-ACT tool for carbon balance, and logistic regression to determine factors influencing CSA adoption.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: CSA/Agroecology practices preserved forest cover, sequestered more carbon, and avoided deforestation relative to conventional practices. EX-ACT analysis estimated large emissions reductions and greater resilience under CSA systems.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA cocoa systems yielded 29% more income per hectare and 37% more cocoa yield than conventional systems. 

Key drivers of CSA adoption included secure tenure, access to extension, and migrant status. 

CSA enhanced farmer resilience through social capital, collective action, and learning. 

Carbon sequestration and GHG mitigation were higher in CSA systems due to afforestation and avoided deforestation.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Small sample size; limited generalizability beyond districts studied; some reliance on self-reported farm data.
publications.NOTES: Supports national REDD+ strategies; provides policy recommendations for farmer support systems and tenure security to enable CSA adoption.

publications.TITLE: Vulnerability of Nigerian maize traders to a confluence of climate, violence, disease and cost shocks
publications.AUTHORS: Carolina M. Vargas, Lenis Saweda O. Liverpool-Tasie, Thomas Reardon
publications.JOURNAL: Journal of Agribusiness in Developing and Emerging Economies
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (North and South regions, with market sampling in Plateau, Kaduna, Kano, Katsina, and Oyo states)
publications.METHODS: Used survey data from 1,100 maize traders (2021) and previous data from 2017; employed probit models to analyze the likelihood and severity of experiencing shocks (climate, violence, COVID-19, price, spoilage) based on trader characteristics (gender, region, firm size, etc.). Included secondary data from ACLED (conflict) and CHIRPS (climate).
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate shocks (e.g., floods, road washouts) are core variables. The paper identifies regional vulnerability patterns and their implications for supply chain disruption and adaptation.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Shocks often occur in tandem, especially price with climate and violence. 

Women are more vulnerable to violence shocks. 

Smaller traders are more vulnerable to price spikes. 

Northern traders face more severe impacts. 

Farming maize buffers price shocks but increases violence exposure.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Correlational not causal; limited ability to quantify income losses; self-reported shock severity; challenges in capturing adaptation strategies.
publications.NOTES: Recommends better infrastructure (roads, rail), finance and insurance tools, and conflict mitigation strategies to protect trader resilience.

publications.TITLE: Strategizing research and development investments in climate change adaptation for root, tuber and banana crops in the African Great Lakes Region: A spatial prioritisation and targeting framework
publications.AUTHORS: Elke Vandamme, Rhys Manners, Julius Adewopo, Graham Thiele, Michael Friedmann, Philip Thornton
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: African Great Lakes Region â€” Burundi, DRC, Kenya, Malawi, Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda
publications.METHODS: The study developed a spatial prioritisation and targeting framework using cluster analysis to identify socio-agroecological homologues (SAHs) based on climate hazards, exposure, and vulnerability. It used biophysical and socio-economic indicators (e.g., HDI, WGI, crop suitability) to guide R&D investment for RT&B crops. Scenario analysis was used to evaluate climate-smart interventions like drought-tolerant varieties and intercropping.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper focuses on climate-related risk and the potential for adaptive interventions (e.g., drought-tolerant varieties, mulching, intercropping) to offset climate stress. Mitigation is not addressed.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Crop suitability varies across the GLR due to climate and socioeconomic factors. 

The proposed SAH framework helps identify regions and crops needing priority investments. 

Scenario modeling shows that targeted technologies can improve crop suitability under future climates.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Socioeconomic data used were contemporary rather than projected. The suitability model assumes ideal planting schedules, which may not reflect real farmer practices.
publications.NOTES: The framework is proposed as a tool for CGIAR and national programs to guide breeding, agronomy, and policy strategies for long-term climate resilience planning.

publications.TITLE: Limited effects of shade on physiological performances of cocoa (Theobroma cacao L.) under elevated temperature
publications.AUTHORS: Eric Opoku Mensah, Richard Asare, Philippe Vaast, Christiana A. Amoatey, Bo Markussen, Kwadwo Owusu, Bismark Kwesi Asitoakor, Anders RÃ¦bild
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana
publications.METHODS: A controlled field experiment assessed the physiological responses of cocoa seedlings under combined shade and heat stress. Using a randomized block design, seedlings were exposed to infra-red heating and/or 60% shading nets. The study evaluated parameters including photosynthesis, chlorophyll fluorescence, chlorophyll content, stomatal density, and biomass. Effects of elevated temperature and shade were modeled using linear mixed effects analysis.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is grounded in climate change as a stressor, assessing temperature thresholds and physiological limits of cocoa.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Elevated temperatures (5â€“7Â°C above ambient) significantly reduced photosynthesis, chlorophyll, and biomass

Shade moderately improved physiological parameters but did not offset heat stress impacts

Optimum temperature for photosynthesis was ~31â€“33Â°C

Shade improved chlorophyll content, leaf area, and water-use efficiency, but these benefits were limited under high heat

Suggests need for shade + other strategies to protect cocoa from extreme temperatures
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Experiment conducted only on seedlings, not mature plants

Artificial heating may not fully replicate field heat stress dynamics

No competition from shade trees was simulated
publications.NOTES:
Funded by DANIDA under the CLIMCOCOA project

Suggests further research in mature agroforestry settings with realistic shade-tree dynamics

Contributes to empirical data gaps on cocoa heat tolerance

publications.TITLE: Identification of Drought, Heat, and Combined Drought and Heat Tolerant Donors in Maize
publications.AUTHORS: Jill E. Cairns, Jose Crossa, P.H. Zaidi, Pichet Grudloyma, Ciro Sanchez, Jose Luis Araus, Suriphat Thaitad, Dan Makumbi, Cosmos Magorokosho, Marianne BÃ¤nziger, Abebe Menkir, Sarah Hearne, Gary N. Atlin
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Eastern and Southern Africa (Kenya, Zimbabwe), Mexico, India, Thailand
publications.METHODS: The study evaluated 300 maize inbred lines from CIMMYT and IITA breeding programs in testcrosses to CML539 across five countries under four stress regimes: drought, heat, combined drought and heat, and optimal conditions. Grain yield, flowering, and plant height were assessed. Broad-sense heritability and genotype-by-environment interactions were analyzed using linear mixed models to identify donors tolerant to combined stresses.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. The study directly responds to IPCC projections of increasing drought and heat stress in Southern Africa and evaluates adaptation traits accordingly.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Maize tolerance to combined drought and heat is genetically distinct from tolerance to each stress alone.

Existing drought-tolerant parents may fail under heat stress.

Identified promising donor lines (e.g., La Posta Seq C7-F64-2-6-2-2 and DTPYC9-F46-1-2-1-2) with high performance under both stressors.

Recommends incorporating combined-stress screening in breeding programs.

Shows that performance under well-watered conditions poorly predicts performance under stress.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Large genotype Ã— trial and residual variances reduce heritability under stress.

Managed stress screening must replicate across trials to improve precision.

More donors are needed as gains in drought tolerance have plateaued.
publications.NOTES:
Study supported by several CGIAR programs (CCAFS, MAIZE) and USAID/Gates Foundation.

Data used to guide donor incorporation into breeding pipelines for SSA.

publications.TITLE: Linking agricultural adaptation strategies, food security and vulnerability: evidence from West Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Sabine Douxchamps, Mark T. Van Wijk, Silvia Silvestri, Abdoulaye S. Moussa, Carlos Quiros, NdeÌ€ye Yacine B. Ndour, Saaka Buah, LÃ©opold SomÃ©, Mario Herrero, Patricia Kristjanson, Mathieu Ouedraogo, Philip K. Thornton, Piet Van Asten, Robert ZougmorÃ©, Mariana C. Rufino
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Burkina Faso, Ghana, Senegal (West Africa)
publications.METHODS: The paper examines how different agricultural adaptation strategies (e.g., crop diversity, small ruminants, SWC, vegetables, fertilizers, improved varieties) relate to food security outcomes in different smallholder household types. It uses survey data from the CCAFS benchmark sites, regression modeling, and typology classification to assess impacts on land productivity, income, and food self-sufficiency.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: This is a climate adaptation study, using CCAFS sites in West Africa to evaluate how household types adopt and benefit from different adaptation practices.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Food security is driven by land productivity, income, and market orientation

Adaptation strategies like soil and water conservation, small ruminants, and crop diversity improve land productivityâ€”especially in more market-oriented households

Households fall into 4 types (subsistence, diversified, extensive, intensified), with distinct strategies and vulnerabilities

Not all households achieve food security through current adaptation strategiesâ€”suggesting the need for transformational change in some cases

Off-farm income plays a key role in stabilizing household cash flows and buffering seasonal vulnerabilities
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Household-level analysis excludes community-level adaptations

Limited to energy-based food security indicators (not nutrition or health)

Adaptation practices may have delayed or context-specific effects
publications.NOTES:
Strong relevance to CCAFS and CGIAR climate-smart agriculture frameworks

Suggests targeting of strategies based on household type rather than blanket promotion

publications.TITLE: Sustainable intensification: What is its role in climate smart agriculture?
publications.AUTHORS: Bruce M. Campbell, Philip Thornton, Robert ZougmoreÌ, Piet van Asten, Leslie Lipper
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with examples from East Africa and the Sahel)
publications.METHODS: This review paper synthesizes the complementary roles of Sustainable Intensification (SI) and Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) using conceptual frameworks and illustrative case studies. It provides a high-level overview of the synergies between CSA objectives (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) and SI practices across different agricultural systems, with examples including coffee-banana intercropping, livestock feed strategies, and agroforestry.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper is explicitly focused on integrating sustainable intensification practices into climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies in agriculture.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
SI and CSA are deeply complementary: CSA helps farmers adapt and builds institutions, while SI boosts productivity and mitigation through efficiency.

Examples include coffeeâ€“banana intercropping in Uganda (IITA-led), livestock feed intensification using Leucaena, and stone bunds and zaÃ¯ pits in the Sahel.

Emphasizes the importance of risk management, ecosystem services, and information systems.

Argues for policies that link adaptation, mitigation, and food system transformation to reduce environmental trade-offs.

SI and CSA are crucial but need to be supported by broader food system reforms, including reducing food waste and promoting better diets.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
CSA and SI often require high upfront costs, which can be barriers to uptake among smallholders.

Risk of intensification leading to land expansion if not accompanied by safeguards

Effectiveness depends on local adaptation and governance systems
publications.NOTES:
The paper features an IITA-led case study on banana-coffee intercropping in East Africa, showing increased income and reduced climate risk.

Strong emphasis on the need for climate justice and support to smallholders who contribute least to climate change but are most affected.

publications.TITLE: Ecological drivers of pteridophyte diversity and distribution in Togo (West Africa)
publications.AUTHORS: Komla Elikplim Abotsi, Ruksan Bose, Kossi Adjossou, Vincent Deblauwe, Germinal Rouhan, Kossi Novinyo Segla, Komina Honam Atsri, Kouami Kokou
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Togo, West Africa
publications.METHODS: Nationwide survey of pteridophyte diversity across 130 plots in 5 ecological zones in Togo. Multivariate analysis, Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA), and indicator species analysis were used to assess influences of climate, land cover, and human disturbance on species richness and composition.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Highlights strong climate influence on pteridophyte distribution. Emphasizes precipitation, elevation, and humidity as key drivers of diversity and resilience, and links species presence to climatic water deficit and cloud cover.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most pteridophyte species are concentrated in submontane humid forests in zone 4 of Togo. 

Climate (especially humidity), land cover, and disturbance significantly affect distribution. 

Agroforests retain ~30% of rainforest diversity. Indicator species vary by land cover and disturbance level.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Plot-based study may not capture full national diversity; ecological indicators based on abundance-dominance may vary across time.
publications.NOTES: Data collected over 5 years; emphasizes need for conserving submontane forests and promoting less destructive agroforestry practices.

publications.TITLE: Farmers, food and climate change: ensuring community-based adaptation is mainstreamed into agricultural programmes
publications.AUTHORS: Helena Wright, Sonja Vermeulen, Gernot Laganda, Max Olupot, Edidah Ampaire, M.L. Jat
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Bangladesh, Mozambique, Uganda, and India
publications.METHODS: Synthesizes insights from four case studies to evaluate the opportunities and barriers for mainstreaming community-based adaptation (CBA) into agricultural programs. Data sources include literature reviews, project evaluations, and participatory research, with an emphasis on extension services and farmer organizations.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong focus on adaptation to climate risks through decentralized governance, participatory development, and policy integration. Climate change is treated as a key cross-sectoral challenge.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Mainstreaming CBA requires enabling environments and institutional innovation. 

Local adaptation knowledge is often not integrated into policy. 

Success depends on bridging top-down and bottom-up processes via extension services, communication, and inclusive governance.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Findings are based on selected case studies; generalizability may be limited. Many adaptation successes remain anecdotal. Policy uptake is often constrained by institutional fragmentation and limited implementation capacity.
publications.NOTES: CBA can mobilize youth and women, as in India, and influence policy reforms. The article argues for procedural justice and long-term institutional embedding of adaptation knowledge rather than one-off projects.

publications.TITLE: Institutional Perspectives of Climate-Smart Agriculture: A Systematic Literature Review
publications.AUTHORS: Edmond Totin, Alcade C. Segnon, Marc Schut, Hippolyte Affognon, Robert B. ZougmorÃ©, Todd Rosenstock, Philip K. Thornton
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (systematic review with emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa, India, Germany, and other regional clusters)
publications.METHODS: This systematic review analyzed 137 peer-reviewed publications from 1991 to 2017 using institutional analysis frameworks. It examined how the CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) are addressed and the extent to which institutional dimensions (e.g., policy, market structure, social norms) are integrated. Thematic and content analysis was conducted using R software and Adobe Acrobat keyword searches based on predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: CSA is framed around productivity, adaptation, and mitigation; adaptation and productivity dominate in lower-income countries, mitigation in higher-income contexts.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA adoption is constrained by weak attention to institutional contexts. 

Market incentives, knowledge infrastructure, and multi-stakeholder partnerships are key to scaling CSA.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: CSA literature lacks focus on physical infrastructure, socio-political and historical contexts, and private sector engagement.
publications.NOTES: Significant gap between CSAâ€™s conceptual framework and its operationalization; stresses the need for institutional innovation to complement technical CSA solutions.

publications.TITLE: A framework for priority-setting in climate-smart agriculture research
publications.AUTHORS: Philip K. Thornton, Anthony Whitbread, Tobias Baedeker, Jill Cairns, Lieven Claessens, Walter Baethgen, Christian Bunn, Michael Friedmann, Ken E. Giller, Mario Herrero, Mark Howden, Kevin Kilcline, Vinay Nangia, Julian Ramirez-Villegas, Shalander Kumar, Paul C. West, Brian Keating
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with an emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
publications.METHODS: The paper develops a conceptual framework for setting research priorities in climate-smart agriculture (CSA). It synthesizes literature, case studies, and expert consultations from a CGIAR-led workshop to define six key elements for CSA priority setting: entry points, scale, research questions, impact estimates for productivity/adaptation/mitigation, social and environmental effects, and likelihood of impact. It reviews tools including cost-benefit analysis, simulation modeling, optimization, and participatory methods.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the article. The framework evaluates both adaptation and mitigation as core CSA pillars, explicitly discussing GHG emission reductions and carbon sequestration.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA prioritization is complex due to trade-offs between adaptation, mitigation, and productivity.

A conceptual six-element framework is proposed to structure CSA research planning.

Impact metrics must consider spatial/temporal scale, stakeholder goals, and system variability.

The framework includes tools like simulation modeling, economic surplus, and participatory ranking.

Equity and social inclusion are identified as important dimensions for future CSA research.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Complexity and data-intensity of tools (e.g., simulation models)

Lack of consensus on CSA definitions

Trade-offs and equity issues remain under-researched
publications.NOTES:
Developed under CGIAR-CCAFS with wide institutional authorship (World Bank, IITA, CIAT, CIMMYT, ILRI)

Focused on research-for-development and donor alignment in CSA inv

publications.TITLE: Horizon scanning to assess the bioclimatic potential for the alien species Spodoptera eridania and its parasitoids after pest detection in West and Central Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, GÃ©rard N. Gouwakinnou, Johannes R. Fagbohoun, Manuele TamÃ², May-Guri SÃ¦thre
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West and Central Africa, with global modeling (especially Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, Latin America)
publications.METHODS: Used species distribution modeling (Maxent) to assess the current and future habitat suitability of Spodoptera eridania (southern armyworm) and four of its co-evolved parasitoids. Climate data from WorldClim was combined with pest and parasitoid occurrence records to generate global suitability maps under current climate and two future climate scenarios (RCP6.0 and RCP8.5). Model accuracy was evaluated with AUC and TSS.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper extensively integrates climate scenarios (RCP6.0 and RCP8.5) but focuses on adaptation through pest forecasting and biological controlâ€”not mitigation.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
S. eridania is expanding in Africa and could severely affect key crops.

Parasitoids like Telenomus remus and Cotesia marginiventris show promise for biocontrol.

Bioclimatic suitability for parasitoids generally overlaps with that of the pest.

Climate change will shift suitability zones, requiring adaptive pest control planning.

Biological control is promoted as a durable, climate-smart pest management approach.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Models do not account for intraguild competition or biotic interactions

Results rely on data availability and quality (e.g., underreporting)

Lack of field validation for parasitoid performance in African contexts
publications.NOTES:
Acknowledges support from CGIAR-CCAFS and World Bank AICCRA project

Builds on previous IITA modeling work on Spodoptera frugiperda

Calls for complementary pest control methods alongside biocontrol

publications.TITLE: Implementation Outline of Climate-Smart One Health
publications.AUTHORS:
Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Henri E. Z. Tonnang,  Stephen Yeboah,  Michael Yao Osae,
Awudu Amadu Gariba, Mustapha Dalaa, Faustina Obeng Adomaa, Osman Tahidu Damba,
Reginald Kyere, FidÃ¨le T. Moutouama, Cyriaque Agboton, Jeannette K. Winsou, Manuele TamÃ², Robert Zougmore
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with emphasis on regions vulnerable to zoonotic disease and climate risk (e.g., Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia)
publications.METHODS: This is a policy-oriented conceptual document that outlines an implementation framework for an integrated One Health approach to climate change adaptation and mitigation. It synthesizes existing frameworks and policies without primary data collection.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The report explicitly centers climate change as both a driver of zoonotic disease and a key threat to food and health systems, outlining mitigation and adaptation strategies within a One Health approach.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
A Climate-Smart One Health approach can address multiple SDGs.

Systems-level coordination across human, animal, and environmental health is critical.

Investments are needed in integrated data, early warning systems, and transdisciplinary capacity building.

Strong emphasis on co-benefits: reduced GHG emissions, improved ecosystem services, and food security.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Challenges to implementation include fragmented governance, weak cross-sector data integration, and limited local capacity.
publications.NOTES:
Developed by a coalition of experts including WHO, FAO, UNEP, and CGIAR.

Recommends pilot projects and scaling through regional coordination platforms.

Intended as an actionable strategy, not a research paper.

publications.TITLE: Global Habitat Suitability of Spodoptera frugiperda (JE Smith) (Lepidoptera, Noctuidae): Key Parasitoids Considered for Its Biological Control
publications.AUTHORS: Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Henri E. Z. Tonnang, Georg Goergen, Sevgan Subramanian, Emily Kimathi, Elfatih M. Abdel-Rahman, Daniel FlÃ¸, Karl H. Thunes, Komi K. M. Fiaboe, Saliou Niassy, Anani Bruce, Samira A. Mohamed, Manuele TamÃ², Sunday Ekesi, May-Guri SÃ¦thre
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with a special focus on Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, and Latin America under current and future (RCP8.5) climate conditions
publications.METHODS: The study used species distribution models (SDMs) to predict the global habitat suitability of Spodoptera frugiperda (fall armyworm) and six of its key parasitoids (biocontrol agents). It employed Maxent modeling based on georeferenced presence records and climatic/environmental predictor variables under current and future climate scenarios (RCP8.5 for 2050). The goal was to inform strategic deployment of biological control for managing fall armyworm globally.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study incorporates climate change (RCP8.5) directly in pest and biocontrol habitat modeling. It focuses on climate adaptation but does not reference mitigation or emissions.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Parasitoids have high habitat overlap with fall armyworm in Africa and parts of Asia and Latin America.

Habitat suitability for both pests and parasitoids may expand or shift under climate change.

Deployment of multiple parasitoids may improve biocontrol robustness.

SDMs offer a cost-effective tool for biocontrol planning across regions.

Fall armyworm poses increasing threat under future climates without effective control.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of native range data for some parasitoids

No field validation of biocontrol efficacy

Habitat suitability does not guarantee field successâ€”ecological interactions are complex
publications.NOTES:
Supported by CGIAR CCAFS and FAW-IPM project

Funded by EU, Royal Norwegian Embassy, and GIZ

IITAâ€™s Biorisk Management Facility (BIMAF) acknowledged as key institutional supportâ€‹

publications.TITLE: How does IPM 3.0 look like (and why do we need it in Africa)?
publications.AUTHORS: Manuele TamÃ², Isabelle Glitho, Ghislain Tepa-Yotto, Rangaswamy Muniappan
