publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (general, with references to Benin, Kenya, Niger, Nigeria, etc.)
publications.METHODS: This is a conceptual review that proposes the next evolution in Integrated Pest Management (IPM 3.0), anchored in three pillars: (1) farmer empowerment through digital decision-making tools, (2) science-based ecological control strategies, and (3) integration of genomics, biopesticides, and habitat management. The article draws on case studies from across Africa to illustrate innovations and policy needs for transitioning away from synthetic pesticide dependency.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is a central context throughout, with IPM 3.0 framed as a tool for resilience and GHG mitigation. Pushâ€“pull, conservation agriculture, and biopesticide use are highlighted as low-emission alternatives.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Farmers need digital, accessible pest ID tools to make informed decisions.

Biopesticides and nature-based pest control can reduce pesticide treadmill.

Pushâ€“pull has evolved to become climate-smart and more drought-resilient.

CRISPR and AI are part of a new generation of ecological pest management.

Policy harmonization and investment in regional diagnostics are essential.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Past IPM approaches failed to reach scale due to complexity and lack of institutional support

Biopesticide uptake remains low due to poor market development and regulation gaps

Gender and literacy barriers still restrict farmer access to digital tools
publications.NOTES:
Promotes a regional early warning system for invasive pests

Funded by USAID, AICCRA/World Bank, and NORAD

Clear linkages to IITA-led innovations like the Farmer Interface Application (FIA)

publications.TITLE: Exploring the floristic diversity of tropical Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Marc S.M. Sosef, Gilles Dauby, Anne Blach-Overgaard, Xander van der Burgt, LuÃ­s Catarino, and 25 others
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tropical Africa (sub-Saharan Africa excluding southern Africa and Madagascar)
publications.METHODS: This large-scale observational study analyzes the RAINBIO databaseâ€”a curated compilation of over 600,000 herbarium records covering 22,577 vascular plant species in tropical Africa. The study uses species richness estimates, floristic turnover metrics, sampling completeness, and growth form classifications to map biodiversity patterns, identify exploration gaps, and suggest priority sampling areas. Analyses were conducted in R using species richness estimators (Chao1, Nielsen), turnover indices (Î²sim), and geospatial modeling.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper emphasizes that up to 90% of species may lose part or all of their suitable climate zones by 2085, underscoring the urgency of conservation under climate change. However, mitigation via carbon or emissions is not addressed.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
RAINBIO contains 609,776 georeferenced herbarium records covering 22,577 species.

Forests in tropical Africa host ~15,387 species, with ~30% endemism in Central Africa.

Sampling remains incomplete; only 21 regions meet high-density standards.

High floristic turnover zones include Cameroon, Gabon, Nimba Mountains, and Eastern Arc.

Strong call for improved digitization and sampling to meet conservation goals.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Many areas lack digitized herbarium data (e.g., Angola, DRC, Republic of Congo)

Data are biased toward regions with higher historical collecting intensity

Some regions remain botanically unexplored despite high conservation importance
publications.NOTES:
Advocates identifying â€œPriority Sampling Unitsâ€ (PSUs) based on high turnover and low sampling

Used to support Objective 1 of the Global Strategy for Plant Conservation 2011â€“2020

Recommends continued support for digitization of African herbaria and database integration

publications.TITLE: Food sovereignty in sub-Saharan Africa: Reality, relevance, and practicality
publications.AUTHORS: Grace Mudombi-Rusinamhodzi and Leonard Rusinamhodzi
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (general)
publications.METHODS: This perspective article critically analyzes the food sovereignty discourse within the socio-ecological realities of sub-Saharan Africa. It reviews agroecological practices, organic and ecological intensification, gender roles, soil fertility, land degradation, yield gaps, and marketing barriers, drawing on published literature and policy experiences to assess practical relevance.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames climate variability as a major constraint in rainfed farming systems and supports ecological intensification as a climate-resilient approach but does not frame any practice as mitigation.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Agroecology alone is insufficient in SSA without integrated inputs and productivity investments.

Food sovereignty is more feasible in high-income settings with supportive markets.

Women face labor-intensive burdens in traditional food systems.

Access to certification, markets, and fair pricing are critical barriers to food system equity.

Food sovereignty must be adapted to local ecological and socio-political contexts.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Food sovereignty is rarely practical in low-yield systems with labor/resource constraints

Organic-only production cannot meet yield requirements in SSA

Certification and premium markets are inaccessible for most smallholders
publications.NOTES:
Advocates integrated soil fertility management (ISFM) and hybrid production models

Suggests that food sovereignty must go beyond activism toward operational policies in SSA

IITA affiliation brings institutional weight to practical policy recommendations

publications.TITLE: Integrating a crop model with a greenhouse gas calculator to identify low carbon agricultural intensification options for smallholder farmers in rural South Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Farirai Rusere, Lynn V. Dicks, Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Olivier Crespo
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: South Africa â€“ specifically Ha Lambani (Limpopo) and Amathole (Eastern Cape)
publications.METHODS: The study integrates two modelsâ€”DSSAT for crop yield simulations and the Cool Farm Tool (CFT) for greenhouse gas calculationsâ€”across various smallholder farm typologies. It evaluates baseline practices and seven conservation agriculture (CA) scenarios to identify those that improve yields while reducing GHG emissions. ANOVA and LSD were used for statistical analysis.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change and low-carbon transitions are central; the study directly links its findings to the Paris Agreement and SDG 2, using emissions modeling and climate-resilient farming practices.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Combining cover crops, residue retention, and no-till boosts yields and reduces emissions.

Single CA practices (e.g., no-till alone) have mixed effectsâ€”best outcomes occur when practices are bundled.

The full CA package (FP + NT + CC + RR) yields the highest benefit in yield and lowest emissions.

Modelling reveals the inefficiency of current practices in both emissions and productivity.

A pathway toward climate-smart intensification is possible even with limited synthetic inputs.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Small sample size of farms used to build typologies

Use of default emission factors may not capture all local variability

Results are more comparative than absolute due to modeling assumptions
publications.NOTES:
Funded by the Water Research Commission and NERC

Links directly to the Paris Agreement and SDG 2

Potentially replicable across other smallholder-dominated regions in SSA

publications.TITLE: Fighting to thrive via plant growth regulators: Green chemical strategies for drought stress tolerance
publications.AUTHORS: Raza A., Iqbal N., Raza Q.A., Zhao X., Wei Y., Jaleel H., et al.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global â€“ no single regional focus, but relevance is emphasized for arid and semi-arid agricultural zones
publications.METHODS: This is a comprehensive narrative review synthesizing physiological and biochemical pathways involved in drought tolerance through plant growth regulators (PGRs), including hormonal crosstalk, osmolyte accumulation, antioxidant systems, and gene expression. It discusses green chemical strategies, mainly focusing on salicylic acid, gibberellins, cytokinins, ABA, and ethylene modulators, without conducting original experiments.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article repeatedly links drought as a primary climate stressor and positions PGRs as tools to manage plant responses under ongoing climate change, particularly for agriculture in arid environments.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought stress causes significant physiological and oxidative damage in plants.

PGRs like salicylic acid, abscisic acid, and cytokinins can improve stress responses.

Green chemicals are safer and more sustainable than synthetic agrochemicals.

Molecular crosstalk between signaling hormones is key for enhancing drought resilience.

Integration into climate-smart farming could reduce yield loss from water stress.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited in vivo validation of some PGR mechanisms

Inconsistent results across species and application rates

Need for large-scale field studies before widespread adoption
publications.NOTES:
Strong advocacy for transitioning to bio-based and sustainable inputs

Discusses global implications, especially for low-rainfall agricultural zones

Proposes molecular markers for future screening of drought tolerance linked to PGR use

publications.TITLE: The Exploitation of Orphan Legumes for Food, Income, and Nutrition Security in Sub-Saharan Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Popoola JO, Aworunse OS, Ojuederie OB, Adewale BD, Ajani OC, Oyatomi OA, Eruemulor DI, Adegboyega TT, Obembe OO
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (West, East, and Southern Africa emphasized)
publications.METHODS: This literature-based review evaluates five underutilized legumes in SSAâ€”African yam bean, Bambara groundnut, Kerstingâ€™s groundnut, Lima bean, and Jack beanâ€”highlighting their agronomic potential, nutritional value, and opportunities for commercialization and genetic improvement. It discusses production constraints, value chains, and the use of modern biotechnological tools like omics and genome editing.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames climate change as a major threat to staple crops and positions orphan legumes as climate-smart alternatives with resilience traits like drought tolerance and soil adaptability.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Orphan legumes offer untapped potential for food security, income, and climate resilience.

Lack of investment, hard-to-cook traits, poor awareness, and absence from trade statistics are barriers.

IITA and partners are conserving genetic resources and promoting advanced breeding tools.

Local markets are growing, but international visibility and formal seed systems remain lacking.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Inadequate policy attention, funding, and research focus on orphan legumes

Lack of production and consumption data

Processing and storage infrastructure are major limitations to scaling
publications.NOTES:
Highlights IITAâ€™s leadership in seed conservation and genomics

Aligns with SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) and 13 (Climate Action)

Strong argument for re-centering neglected legumes in food systems

publications.TITLE: Integrating temperature-dependent life table data into Insect Life Cycle Model for predicting the potential distribution of Scapsipedus icipe Hugel & Tanga
publications.AUTHORS: Magara H.J. Otieno, Monica A. Ayieko, Saliou Niassy, Daisy Salifu, Azrag G.A. Abdelmutalab, Khamis M. Fathiya, Sevgan Subramanian, Komi K.M. Fiaboe, Nana Roos, Sunday Ekesi, Chrysantus M. Tanga
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Africa â€“ with model simulations for Eastern, Western, Central, and parts of Southern Africa
publications.METHODS: The study developed and validated temperature-dependent demographic models for Scapsipedus icipe, an edible cricket species, using life table parameters measured at 9 constant temperatures. The models were integrated into the Insect Life Cycle Modeling (ILCYM) platform to project spatial distribution across Africa under current and future climate scenarios. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, regression modeling, and spatial simulations using WorldClim data and IPCC climate projections.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is explicitly integrated into modeling scenarios using 2050 projections, guiding adaptive food system development for insect-based protein.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal growth of S. icipe occurs at 30Â°C; development fails at 10Â°C and 40Â°C.

Highest fecundity and population growth were recorded at moderate temperatures, offering clear insights for production system design.

Spatial simulations predict broad potential for establishment across Sub-Saharan Africa.

Introduces life-cycle-based conservation mapping for new edible species.

Demonstrates the utility of ILCYM in informing insect production and climate adaptation planning.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Based on constant temperature regimes; real-world variability not captured

Focused only on thermal factors, excluding humidity and resource limitations

No socioeconomic data on production feasibility or consumer acceptance
publications.NOTES:
Funded by Danida, IDRC, ACIAR, and BioInnovate through icipe

Provides a replicable modeling approach for other new insect species

Suggests insect farming as part of African climate resilience and nutrition strategies

publications.TITLE: Genomic Analysis of Selected Maize Landraces from Sahel and Coastal West Africa Reveals Their Variability and Potential for Genetic Enhancement
publications.AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Ana LuÃ­sa Garcia-Oliveira, Antonia Tetteh, Agre Paterne, Assanvo Simon-Pierre Nâ€™guetta, Melaku Gedil
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo)
publications.METHODS:
Genotyped 208 maize accessions (190 landraces and 18 reference populations) using 5974 high-quality SNP markers from DArTseq.

Diversity indices (He, Ho, PIC, MAF) were estimated using PowerMarker.

Population structure was inferred using STRUCTURE (K = 1 to 12), supported by DAPC, PCoA, and NJ tree clustering.

AMOVA partitioned genetic variation within and between populations; FST and Nm were calculated for differentiation and gene flow.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames landrace diversity as essential for developing maize that can withstand drought and heat, both of which are increasing due to climate changeâ€”emphasizing adaptation but not mitigation.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Three distinct gene pools identified; Burkinabe gene pool was the most genetically differentiated.

High within-population variation (86%) and moderate among-population variation (14%).

Landraces show strong potential for improving stress tolerance in maize.

Reference populations were genetically similar to Ghanaian and Togolese landraces, but not to Burkinabe.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Possible misclassification of old improved varieties as landraces

Lack of eco-geographical data for many accessions

Genetic admixture due to historical germplasm exchange

Sampling bias due to historical collection methods
publications.NOTES:
Suggests Burkinabe gene pool as a priority source for heat and drought tolerance

Supports future GWAS and marker-assisted selection

Data deposited at IITA repository and supported by the STMA project and WASCAL

publications.TITLE: Exploring farmerâ€™s assessment of soil quality and root yield in cassava-based cropping systems
publications.AUTHORS: Samuel Ayodele Mesele, Paul Sobowale Soremi, James Kehinde Adigun
publications.JOURNAL: Journal of the Saudi Society of Agricultural Sciences
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southwestern Nigeria (Ogun State, specifically Wasimi village)
publications.METHODS: The study used participatory methods (interviews, group discussions, field observations) with 40 cassava farmers to explore indigenous indicators of soil quality and yield estimation. It compared farmersâ€™ yield predictions with actual harvest data and conducted laboratory soil analyses of four representative farm plots. A linear regression was used to compare predicted vs. measured yield.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Farmers attribute yield decline primarily to reduced rainfall. Adaptive practices include early planting and crop diversification.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Farmers use soil colour, drainage, and depth to assess land suitability

Visual yield indicators include soil cracks and leaf/stem appearance

Cassava yield has declined 10â€“50% over the past decade due to low rainfall and poor soils

Farmersâ€™ yield estimates moderately correlate with actual yields (RÂ² = 0.62)

Soil analyses confirmed low fertility across study sites

Integrating traditional knowledge with scientific metrics can strengthen agricultural interventions
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weather data were unavailable to validate rainfall-related yield trends

Yield estimation accuracy varied widely among farmers

Soil data were limited to four farm sites
publications.NOTES:
Strong emphasis on farmer inclusion and participatory research

No external funding was received for the research

Study underscores missed opportunities when farmer knowledge is excluded from intervention design

publications.TITLE: Framework for Incorporating Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) Elements in Climate Information Services (CIS)
publications.AUTHORS: Everisto Mapedza, Sophia Huyer, Nitya Chanana, Alison Rose, Inga Jacobs-Mata, Netsayi Noris Mudege, Sabine Homann-Kee Tui, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Gloriose Nsengiyumva, Munyaradzi Mutenje, Ngowenani Nohayi
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Zambia (with reference to Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Mali, and Senegal under AICCRA)
publications.METHODS: The paper uses a literature review approach to propose a gender equality and social inclusion (GESI) framework for Climate Information Services (CIS), grounded in the ongoing AICCRA project. The framework is structured around five core indicators: gender targeting by design, sex-disaggregated data collection and analysis, dissemination of technology, and continuous gender monitoring and empowerment evaluation.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability and its disproportionate impacts on women are the foundation for the GESI-CIS framework. The goal is to make adaptation tools more accessible and equitable.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Women are systematically excluded from CIS due to socio-cultural, institutional, and systemic barriers

Gendered access to communication technologies is critical for effective CIS delivery

Framework operationalized in Zambia via agricultural data hubs, flood/drought indicators, and training modules

Five indicators proposed to track inclusion: targeting, data, analysis, dissemination, and empowerment evaluation

Emphasizes transformative change beyond inclusionâ€”targeting structural gender norms
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
CIS alone is insufficient to empower womenâ€”norms and structural inequalities must also be addressed

Context-specific challenges in each country require flexible framework adaptation
publications.NOTES:
Funded by the World Bank via AICCRA (P173398)

Builds directly on CGIAR-CCAFS legacy research

Highlights digital inequality and the intersectionality of gender with age, class, literacy, and disability

publications.TITLE: Interactive effects of altitude, microclimate and shading system on coffee leaf rust
publications.AUTHORS: Theresa Liebig, Fabienne Ribeyre, Peter LÃ¤derach, Hans-Michael Poehling, Piet van Asten, Jacques Avelino
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda (Mt. Elgon region: Bulambuli, Kapchorwa, Sironko districts)
publications.METHODS: This study examined how altitude and coffee shading systems influence the microclimate and Coffee Leaf Rust (CLR) incidence in Arabica coffee agroecosystems in Uganda. Field data (e.g., leaf rust incidence, temperature, RH, dew point) were collected over a growing season across 49 plots at three altitudes and with varying shade configurations. The authors used piecewise structural equation modeling (SEM) to assess causal relationships between environmental factors, shading systems, microclimate, and CLR development.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong climate-relevance: connects microclimatic variation across altitudes and seasons with Coffee Leaf Rust severity. Climate change implications for disease outbreaks are implied.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
CLR incidence is significantly influenced by altitude, shade system, and microclimate (e.g., dew point, night temperature, RH)

Higher altitudes and certain shade configurations (e.g., coffee-tree systems) are associated with lower CLR incidence

Shade effects on microclimate are complex and vary with altitude

Diurnal temperature range and humidity are key disease drivers

Shade system effects may mitigate or exacerbate CLR depending on environmental context
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Did not assess yield or socio-economic outcomes

Study area-specific results may not generalize across all agroecological zones

Other biotic and abiotic interactions not included in final model
publications.NOTES:
Funded by BMZ and implemented under CCAFS

Collaborators include IITA, CIAT, CIRAD, and local farmers

Avelino and van Asten are long-standing contributors to coffee agroecology and systems disease management

publications.TITLE: How future climatic uncertainty and biotic stressors might influence the sustainability of African vegetable production
publications.AUTHORS: J.D.H. Keatinge, D.R. Ledesma, J.dâ€™A. Hughes, F.J.D. Keatinge, S. Hauser, P.C.S. Traore
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Pan-African (locations include Nigeria, Kenya, Mali, Tunisia, etc.)
publications.METHODS: This review analyzes long-term temperature and rainfall data (1975â€“2014 and up to 100 years where available) across 13 African countries. It assesses climate trends and predicts future warming scenarios. The paper focuses on how increasing temperatures and climate variability will impact biotic stressors (pests, diseases, weeds) in African vegetable production systems, especially for smallholder farmers.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Temperature trends are projected for 25â€“100 years, and future risks to vegetable production are linked to increased pests, diseases, and weed pressures.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate change will exacerbate crop losses via biotic stressors

Rainfall trends remain highly variable and statistically insignificant

Localized responses neededâ€”climate trends vary by site

Temperature rise projected up to +6.87Â°C by 2115 in Tunisia; +1.11Â°C in Ibadan (IITA)

Highlights the need for temperature-adapted breeding and disease monitoring

Policy and funding should prioritize adaptive horticulture and farmer resilience
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Long-term rainfall data were scarce and trends were inconclusive

Highly variable local conditions limit regional generalizability

Did not include field-level pest or crop performance data
publications.NOTES:

Supports Sustainable Development Goal 2 via improved horticulture

IITA and AVRDC data sources contributed to the climate analysis

publications.TITLE: Habitat range shift and prediction of the potential future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii (Baill.) Heckel in Benin (West Africa)
publications.AUTHORS: Guillaume Hounsou-Dindin, Rodrigue Idohou, Paterne Agre, Achille HounkpÃ¨vi, Aristide Cossi Adomou, Achille Ephrem Assogbadjo, Romain GlÃ¨lÃ¨ KakaÃ¯
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Benin (with emphasis on humid and sub-humid zones)
publications.METHODS: Species distribution modeling (SDM) using an ensemble of four algorithms (RF, GBM, GLM, MAXENT) in the biomod2 framework was applied to current and projected climate and soil data (RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios) to assess the present and future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii in Benin. Occurrence records were collected from field sampling and GBIF, and a predictive model was developed to identify suitable conservation and domestication zones.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios are used to project shifts in suitable habitat zones through 2085.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Currently, 4.43% of Benin is highly suitable for R. heudelotii

Under both RCP scenarios, humid zone habitat suitability declines, while sub-humid zones gain importance

Model performance was very high (AUC = 0.994; TSS = 0.925)

Environmental variables (isothermality, rainfall, and soil sand content) are key predictors

Results inform national planning for climate-smart forest and agroforestry management
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No modeling of biotic interactions, dispersal, or landscape connectivity

SDM limitations: does not predict actual presence, only suitability

No socioeconomic constraints modeled (e.g., land access, institutional barriers)
publications.NOTES:
Recommends ex situ strategies (seedling propagation, assisted migration)

Important case study for climate-resilient agroforestry planning in West Africa

publications.TITLE: Exâ€ante evaluation of promising soybean innovations for subâ€Saharan Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, Arega Alene, Alpha Kamara, Keith Wiebe, Victor Manyong, Tahirou Abdoulaye, Petros Mkandawire
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (including country-level focus on Nigeria, Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Uganda, Benin, Ethiopia, Cameroon, CÃ´te d'Ivoire, Gabon, etc.)
publications.IITA OFFICE: IITA Malawi, IITA Nigeria, IITA Tanzania
publications.METHODS: The study uses a combination of crop modeling, hydrology modeling, and the IMPACT economic model to assess how soybean innovations (e.g., improved varieties, increased acreage, and expanded processing capacity) may reduce import dependency and enhance production in sub-Saharan Africa under future climate scenarios.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the modeling exercise. Yield impacts under RCP 8.5 are used to define risks and adaptation needs. The HadGEM2-ES model is used to simulate future drought-related stress.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
SSAâ€™s soybean import dependency is projected to worsen without intervention

Yield-enhancing tech alone insufficient; acreage and processing must also double

Combined innovations could make SSA self-sufficient in soybean by 2050

High-value recommendations for policy (e.g., subsidies, floor prices, contract farming)

Supported by CGIAR-PIM and Gates Foundation

publications.TITLE: Rhizobium inoculant, nitrogen, and phosphorus improves photosynthesis and water use efficiency in soybean under drought stress
publications.AUTHORS: Singh, M., Singh, V., Khera, D. et al.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
publications.METHODS: Greenhouse pot experiments were conducted using soybean varieties, applying rhizobium inoculation, nitrogen, and phosphorus treatments. Photosynthesis and water use efficiency were measured under induced drought and well-watered conditions to assess physiological responses.
publications.IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper investigates physiological mechanisms (photosynthesis, water use) through which soybean can become more drought-resilient, explicitly positioning the research within climate adaptation.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Inoculation with rhizobium plus nitrogen and phosphorus significantly enhanced photosynthesis and water use efficiency.

The combined treatment improved drought tolerance in soybean, suggesting a viable strategy to sustain productivity under climate stress.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Greenhouse study may not fully replicate field conditions; long-term effects not evaluated.
publications.NOTES: Strong physiological focus with clear implications for adaptation but not broader food system themes.

publications.TITLE: Forest governance and REDD+ in Central Africa: Towards a participatory model to increase stakeholder involvement in carbon markets
publications.AUTHORS: Adrien N. Djomo, J. Andrew Grant, Celestine Fonyikeh-Bomboh Lucha, Julie Tchoko Gagoe, NoÃ«l H. Fonton, Neal Scott, Denis Jean Sonwa
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Africa (Congo Basin countries: Cameroon, DRC, Gabon, Republic of Congo, Central African Republic, Equatorial Guinea)
publications.METHODS: The paper provides a literature-based and policy review of REDD+ initiatives in the Congo Basin, analyzing carbon stock monitoring, governance structures, stakeholder participation, and implementation challenges. It includes historical data synthesis, silvicultural system design, and proposed carbon accounting methodologies. The authors propose a participatory model for engaging local communities in carbon markets.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper centers on REDD+ as a climate mitigation mechanism and emphasizes the need for equitable governance, community incentives, and carbon accounting to effectively reduce deforestation in the Congo Basin.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
REDD+ must go beyond technical monitoring and involve participatory governance and local rights.

Accurate carbon accounting and forest reference levels are essential for access to carbon markets.

Community forestry, when legally recognized and properly funded, can yield carbon and livelihood benefits.

Forest degradation persists due to weak governance, insecure tenure, and lack of local incentives.

The authors propose a 30-year silvicultural system with regular inventories to improve forest management and carbon sequestration.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of funding mechanisms and secure land tenure in REDD+ implementation

Inadequate inclusion of bryophytes and non-tree biomass in current carbon accounting

Challenges with bureaucratic complexity and corruption in forest governance
publications.NOTES:
The paper highlights IITAâ€™s and CIFORâ€™s roles in REDD+ policy development and research in Central Africa.

Authors stress that mitigation success requires legally binding tenure systems, simplified rules, and full local participation.

publications.TITLE: Historical environmental change in Africa drives divergence and admixture of Aedes aegypti mosquitoes: a precursor to successful worldwide colonization?
publications.AUTHORS: Kelly Louise Bennett, Fortunate Shija, Yvonne-Marie Linton, Gerald Misinzo, Martha Kaddumukasa, Rousseau Djouaka, Okorie Anyaele, Angela Harris, Seth Irish, Thaung Hlaing, Anil Prakash, Julius Lutwama, Catherine Walton
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West, Central, and East Africa (specifically Nigeria, Benin, Uganda, Tanzania, Kenya, and Ivory Coast)
publications.METHODS: The study used genetic data (four nuclear and one mitochondrial marker) from Aedes aegypti mosquitoes collected across 19 sites in Africa and 17 global tropical locations. Through Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC), it tested demographic models to evaluate population structure, historical admixture, divergence, and colonization pathways. Findings were used to understand how environmental change shaped genetic structure and facilitated global invasion.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study links forest fragmentation and expansion due to Pleistocene and Holocene climate shifts to genetic divergence and admixture in Aedes aegypti. These evolutionary processes increased the mosquitoesâ€™ adaptive potential for human environments and global spread.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Historical climatic changes led to population divergence and secondary contact among Aedes aegypti populations in African forest refugia.

Genetic admixture during the Holocene facilitated the emergence of traits suitable for domestic environments.

Evidence supports a single out-of-Africa colonization event with decreased genetic diversity from Africa to the Americas and Asia.

The study highlights the importance of evolutionary history in vector control and disease prediction efforts.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Inability to definitively determine colonization route due to signal masking from admixture

ABC models are simplified and may not fully capture the complexity of evolutionary history

Variation in informativeness across genetic markers
publications.NOTES:
The study improves understanding of vector adaptation and informs strategies for managing disease risk amid climate change.

It emphasizes the interplay between environmental change and biological invasion.

publications.TITLE: A global meta-analysis of soil organic carbon in the Anthropocene
publications.AUTHORS: Damien Beillouin, Marc Corbeels, Julien Demenois, David Berre, Annie Boyer, Abigail Fallot, FrÃ©dÃ©ric Feder, RÃ©mi Cardinael
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: N/a
publications.METHODS: This second-order meta-analysis synthesized results from 230 first-order meta-analyses (25,000+ primary studies) to evaluate the effects of land-use change, land management, and climate change (direct and indirect) on soil organic carbon (SOC). Statistical comparisons were performed using both frequentist and Bayesian methods. The study assessed percent change in SOC by driver type and across land-use categories (croplands, forests, grasslands, wetlands, etc.).
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explores both the direct (e.g., warming, COâ‚‚ enrichment) and indirect (e.g., wildfire, snow cover change) effects of climate change on SOC, finding that indirect effects may have greater SOC impacts than direct ones.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Land-use change and land management have a 7â€“10x larger impact on SOC than direct climate change effects.

Forest and wetland conversion to cropland leads to major SOC losses.

SOC gains can be achieved through agroforestry (+20â€“33%), biochar (+67%), and organic amendments (+29â€“34%).

SOC impacts vary significantly by land-use type; cropland has the highest potential for SOC gain.

Few studies focus on wetlands or indirect climate effectsâ€”highlighting major research gaps.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Some ecosystem types (e.g., wetlands) are underrepresented in meta-analyses

Most studies are short-term and may not capture long-term SOC dynamics

Results may not generalize well to all local contexts due to pedoclimatic variability
publications.NOTES:
The article is a major contribution to the evidence base for global climate mitigation through soil carbon.

Supports policies promoting nature-based solutions, especially in agriculture and land management.

publications.TITLE: Influences of climate variability on cocoa health and productivity in agroforestry systems in Ghana
publications.AUTHORS: Bismark Kwesi Asitoakor, Richard Asare, Anders RÃ¦bild, Hans Peter Ravn, Vincent Yao Eziah, Kwadwo Owusu, Eric Opoku Mensah, Philippe Vaast
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Southern, Middle, and Northern cocoa belts)
publications.METHODS: Longitudinal study (2016â€“2019) across 23 cocoa farms in Ghana's cocoa belts. Monitored 460 cocoa trees for canopy health, flowering, pest/disease damage, and yield. Used rainfall gauges, temperature loggers, and structured observations. Employed linear mixed models to assess effects of climatic factors, shade tree characteristics, and farm practices on productivity.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Study focuses on how climate gradients (rainfall and temperature) affect cocoa health and productivity. High rainfall enhances canopy health and flowering; higher temperatures increase pest incidence. Findings support climate-smart shading and management practices.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cocoa productivity was highest in the wetter southern cocoa belt and lowest in the drier north. 

Black pod disease was most common in humid areas, while mirids and shield bugs were more prevalent in dry zones. 

Larger crown area of shade trees improved canopy health but also increased black pod risk. 

Distance from shade trees had mixed effects on flowering and pest damage. 

Farm management practices significantly influenced outcomes.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: No soil moisture data collected; complex interactions between shade trees, pests, and climate require further study; results may not fully generalize outside Ghana.
publications.NOTES: Highlights role of climate-smart agroforestry and pruning practices. Recommends careful selection of shade tree species to balance benefits and risks.

publications.TITLE: Gender in climate change, agriculture, and natural resource policies: insights from East Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Edidah L. Ampaire, Mariola Acosta, Sofia Huyer, Ritah Kigonya, Perez Muchunguzi, Rebecca Muna, Laurence Jassogne
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda and Tanzania
publications.METHODS: Content analysis of 155 agricultural and natural resource policies, development plans, and action plans from national, district, and sub-county/ward levels. Gender budgeting review based on district and sub-county budgets from 2012â€“2016. Evaluation based on degree of gender integration, budget allocations, and stakeholder workshops.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on institutional and policy readiness for gender-sensitive climate action. Reviews how national and sub-national policies integrate gender to support climate change resilience and adaptation in agriculture and natural resources.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Gender integration in policies has improved since the 1990s, but major gaps remain in execution, budgeting, and structural analysis. 

Most policies treat gender as a 'womenâ€™s issue' and fail to address menâ€™s vulnerabilities or intersectionality.

Budget allocations to gender are minimal and inconsistent, with little funding reaching structural interventions. 

Stakeholders reported capacity gaps and low technical ability to apply gender-responsive budgeting.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Focuses only on two countries and four years of budget data. Analysis based on publicly accessible policy documents and stakeholder feedback.
publications.NOTES: The study is part of the CGIAR CCAFS program. It recommends clearer national frameworks for gender mainstreaming and budget tracking. Includes a 5-point grading rubric for gender integration in policy documents and a proposal for harmonized gender strategies across levels of governance.

publications.TITLE: Gender and impact of climate change adaptation on soybean farmersâ€™ revenue in rural Togo, West Africa
publications.AUTHORS: Essossinam Ali, NadÃ¨ge Essossolim Awade, Tahirou Abdoulaye
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Region, Togo (Tchamba, Sotouboua, and Blitta districts)
publications.METHODS: Cross-sectional survey of 500 soybean farmers analyzed using endogenous switching regression. Evaluated gender-differentiated impacts of climate change adaptation on farm revenue, considering access to extension, credit, training, and land ownership.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate change adaptation through strategies like crop rotation, seed selection, adjusted sowing, and agroforestry. Assesses impacts of these strategies under rising temperature and declining rainfall patterns in Togo.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Only 40.37% of women adopted climate adaptation strategies compared to 59.62% of men. 

Women who adapted had higher soybean revenue than men, but women who did not adapt had significantly lower revenue. 

Key drivers of adaptation included access to extension, credit, education, and farmer-based organizations. 

Non-adaptation led to a 0.268% decrease in revenue. 

Use of crop rotation and access to credit significantly increased revenue.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Cross-sectional data may not capture seasonal effects; limited to three districts; does not account for long-term effects of adaptation choices.
publications.NOTES: Study recommends gender mainstreaming in climate adaptation policies. Supported by AGRA, IDRC, CORAF/WECARD, and IITA.

publications.TITLE: Gender-responsive approaches to rapid climate warming among smallholder farmers
publications.AUTHORS: Abraham Akyala, Ayansina Ayanlade, Olajumoke Adeyeye, Isaac A. Oluwatimilehin
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nasarawa State, Nigeria
publications.METHODS: Used 36-year temperature dataset (1984â€“2020) from Nigeria Meteorological Agency and primary survey of 52 purposively selected smallholder farmers (26 men, 26 women) using semi-structured questionnaires and in-depth interviews. Data analyzed with Mann-Kendall trend test, descriptive stats, and thematic coding of interview data.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on rapid climate warming trends and gender-differentiated perceptions and responses among smallholder farmers. Uses observed temperature trends and self-reported adaptation strategies to explore differential vulnerability and resilience.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Statistical trend in increasing max temperature confirmed rapid warming. 

Women adopted traditional irrigation (66%) and cover cropping (75%) more often; men favored sustainable forestry (74%). 

Gender norms, land rights, and access to resources significantly shaped adaptation strategies. 

Education, income, and institutional support were all impacted by warming. 

Womenâ€™s proximity to natural resources increased climate awareness, but they faced constraints in land access and support services.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Small sample size; localized to Nasarawa State; relies on self-reported adaptation behavior and perception data.
publications.NOTES: Recommends integrating gender into climate finance and adaptive capacity-building. Gender-informed policies should support womenâ€™s knowledge, access, and innovation.

publications.TITLE: A Global Assessment of the State of Plant Health
publications.AUTHORS: Serge Savary et al., Global Plant Health Assessment Project (GPHA)
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with specific focus on regions including Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and others
publications.IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Kenya, IITA Cameroon, IITA Tanzania
publications.METHODS: Collaborative expert-driven global assessment of plant health across 33 plant system Ã— ecoregion combinations. Synthesized evidence from literature and expert reports using a structured framework based on the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment. Included evaluations of provisioning, regulating, and cultural ecosystem services and their trends.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Extensively addresses the impact of climate change on plant health, emphasizing interactions between climate variability, pathogen invasions, and phytobiome dynamics in agrosystems and natural ecosystems.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most plant systems assessed are in fair or poor health, with many in decline. 

Major drivers include climate change, human activity, and pathogen invasions. 

The state of plant health is especially poor in Sub-Saharan Africa, contributing to food insecurity and environmental degradation. 

The report advocates for improved data, collective action, and resilience strategies such as genetic diversity and ecosystem-based management.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Relies on expert assessments due to lack of quantitative global data; plant health considered mainly through lens of infectious disease; ecological complexity and phytobiome dynamics not fully integrated.
publications.NOTES: Coordinated by the International Society for Plant Pathology and supported by IITA and global institutions; structured around the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment framework; emphasizes need for multidisciplinary research and policy engagement.

publications.TITLE: Indigenous African Orphan Legumes: Potential for Food and Nutrition Security in SSA
publications.AUTHORS: Michael Abberton, Rajneesh Paliwal, Benjamin Faloye, Tchamba Marimagne, Azeez Moriam, Olaniyi Oyatomi
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa
publications.METHODS: Review of literature on African orphan legumes with emphasis on genetic resources, breeding methods, and nutritional and agronomic value.

genomic tools hold promise for improvement

publications.TITLE: Global agricultural intensification during climate change: a role for genomics
publications.AUTHORS: Michael Abberton, Jacqueline Batley, Alison Bentley, John Bryant, Hongwei Cai, James Cockram, Antonio Costa de Oliveira, Leland J. Cseke, et al.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (broad scope; includes examples from Asia, Africa, Europe)

publications.METHODS: This is a narrative literature review synthesizing current knowledge on genomic-assisted breeding and its role in increasing food production resilience to climate change. It presents a high-level synthesis of genomics applications and stresses the urgency of investment in genomic tools to address crop vulnerabilities globally.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
publications.ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is framed as a major driver necessitating a â€œsecond Green Revolution.â€ The paper emphasizes rising global temperatures, altered rainfall, increased pests, and greater yield instability. Genomics is presented as a critical tool for building resilience through new breeding strategies.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate stressors threaten current food production models.

Genomic-assisted breeding is essential for climate-resilient crops.

Minor/orphan crops should receive more attention.

Genomic tools can expand crop diversity, improve stability, and promote food security.
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:

Breeding gains may take decades to fully materialize.

Limited progress in applying genomics to orphan crops and marginalized regions.

publications.TITLE: Biological control of aflatoxins in Africa: current status and potential challenges in the face of climate change
publications.AUTHORS: R. Bandyopadhyay, A. Ortega-Beltran, A. Akande, C. Mutegi, J. Atehnkeng, L. Kaptoge, A.L. Senghor, B.N. Adhikari, P.J. Cotty
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (with specific references to Nigeria, Kenya, Senegal, The Gambia, Ghana, Zambia, Malawi, Tanzania, Uganda, Mozambique, and Burkina Faso)
publications.IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Kenya, IITA Malawi
publications.METHODS: This paper synthesizes policy, technical, and institutional experiences from the development and scaling of Aflasafe in Africa. It includes discussions of efficacy data, regulatory processes, and implementation models, especially in response to climate change risks.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Discusses increasing aflatoxin risk due to climate change and how Aflasafe technology can help mitigate these impacts.

Native atoxigenic fungi are essential for ecological safety and effectiveness.

Climate change is increasing the urgency for aflatoxin management.

publications.TITLE: Comparative Multi-Criteria Assessment of Climate Policies and Sustainable Development Strategies in Cameroon: Towards a GIS Decision-Support Tool for the Design of an Optimal REDD+ Strategy
publications.AUTHORS: Anderson Gwanyebit Kehbila, Dieudonne Alemagi, Peter Akong Minang
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (national-level, Congo Basin relevance)
publications.METHODS: This study uses a multi-criteria analysis framework to evaluate five key national policies in Cameroon for their compatibility with REDD+ objectives. The authors develop and propose a GIS-based decision-support system (GIS-REDD+DSS) to strengthen adaptive REDD+ planning. Policies assessed include PRSP, GESP, FESP, Energy Policy, and the First National Communication on Climate Change. Criteria were developed from global REDD+ and sustainable development frameworks.
publications.IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
publications.NOTES ON CLIMATE: Central to the study. It assesses national policy coherence with REDD+ goals and presents an adaptive, GIS-based decision framework for carbon emissions reductions, forest protection, and land-use planning.
publications.THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most policies (PRSP, FESP, Energy Policy, etc.) lack clear REDD+ targets or safeguards

PRSP and GESP align with poverty and sustainable development but lack climate planning integration

FNCCC1 includes mitigation/adaptation but lacks operationalization

GIS-DSS model includes databases, valuation tools, and a virtual REDD+ marketplace

Urges policy harmonization across sectors for effective REDD+ rollout in Cameroon and Congo Basin countries
publications.LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Subjectivity in interpreting policies

Lack of concrete implementation measures across assessed policies

DSS effectiveness depends on national capacity and data availability

publications.TITLE: A stepwise approach to facilitate adoption of climate smart practices for smallholder coffee production in Uganda
publications.AUTHORS: Mukasa, D., Rusinamhodzi, L., van Asten, Piet. J. A., Amwonya, D., Sseguya, H., Okiror, F. A., … Jassogne, L.
publications.GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda
publications.METHODS: The details of study design are described elaborated by the theoretical basis for the Stepwise approach. The study considered action research and case study research design. It consisted of three main components that helped to shape the Stepwise logic: (a) co-design and co-development, (b) experiential learning, (c) farmers’ feedback, and (d) Hypothesis needed for field testing to ascertain its validity and practicality. The combination of these brought about the idea of breaking down the GAPs packages into smaller packages that could be implemented in phases. The process of development of the approach, tools, experiential learning, farmers’ feedback, assumptions, and data underpinning Stepwise approach.
publications.ABSTRACT: Adoption of Climate-smart Agricultural Practices (CSA) enhances crop productivity and livelihoods. Smallholder coffee farmers in Uganda receive training on good agricultural practices (GAPs) through extension programs, but adoption remains low. Adopting all ‘best practices’ at once may be unrealistic for resource-limited, risk-averse smallholders. This study tested a Stepwise approach, breaking GAPs into manageable, incremental investments. Field trials were conducted in Luweero (Central) and Sironko (East) districts of Uganda, with 16 demonstration sites using randomized treatments and the control (farmer practice), divided into four steps. Each site served 25–30 farmers through experiential learning. A co-design method ensured farmer involvement in the design, development, and testing for sustainability. Results from two harvests (2018–2019) showed significant cumulative yield gains over the control. Arabica yields increased by 31%, 43%, 54% and 65% across Steps 1-4,. Robusta showed gains of 7%, 22%, 23% and 39% respectively. Marginal rate of returns (MRR) was relatively high for Step 1 (563%), 2 (169%) and 4 (122%) for Robusta coffee , and 221%, 217% and 485% for Step 2, 3 and 4 for Arabica coffee respectively. The Stepwise approach demonstrated improved yield gains and increased farmer income.
publications.KEYWORDS: Coffee, productivity, GAPs, MRR, Stepwise approach, Uganda
